subject: Electrotherapy I
definition: Electro-Diagnostics: The use of electrical stimulation to assess the excitability, integrity, and functional status of peripheral nerves and skeletal muscles.

Strength-Duration (S-D) Curve: A graphical plot representing the relationship between the intensity of an electrical stimulus (strength) and the pulse duration required to elicit a threshold response in excitable tissue.
Rheobase: The minimum current intensity (measured in mA or Volts) of infinite duration (clinically represented by 100 ms or 300 ms pulses) required to produce a threshold visible muscle contraction.
Chronaxie: The minimum pulse duration (measured in milliseconds) required to elicit a threshold muscle contraction when applying a current intensity exactly double the rheobase value.
Accommodation Index: The ratio of the threshold current required to elicit muscle contraction using slow-rising (triangular) pulses to that required using rapid-rising (rectangular) pulses.
Galvanic-Faradic Test: A classical qualitative electrodiagnostic test comparing a muscle's response to long-duration galvanic (100 ms) and short-duration Faradic (1 ms) current.
principle: Axonal vs. Myofibrillar Excitability: Healthy myelinated motor axons have a highly sensitive threshold and very short chronaxie (0.01 to 0.5 ms). Denervated muscle fibers lack myelin insulation, raising their threshold and shifting their chronaxie to much longer durations (10 to 50 ms).

S-D Curve Shift in Denervation: Nerve injury shifts the S-D curve upward and to the right, reflecting loss of myelin axons and higher excitation thresholds. In partial denervation, a 'kink' appears in the curve, indicating a mix of innervated and denervated fibers.

Accommodation Principle: Motor nerves accommodate easily to slow-rising currents, whereas denervated muscle membranes have minimal accommodation capacity, allowing them to depolarize in response to slow-rising triangular pulses.
equipment: Diagnostic Electrodiagnostic Stimulator (Capable of switching between constant current CC and constant voltage CV, and delivering pulse durations from 0.01 ms to 100-300 ms)
Precise Active Diagnostic Pen Electrode (with a built-in current trigger key)
Graph Template Sheet or digital plotting interface

parameters.normalChronaxieRange: 0.05 ms to 0.5 ms (for healthy innervated muscle-nerve units)
parameters.denervatedChronaxieRange: 10 ms to 50 ms (for denervated skeletal muscle tissue)

mechanism: Single pulse depolarizes nerve or muscle membrane above threshold.
Initiates an isolated, synchronous contraction (twitch) of the motor unit.
Short pulses (<1 ms) selectively excite highly excitable myelinated motor nerves.
Long pulses (>10 ms) bypass degenerated nerve axon block to directly excite denervated sarcolemma.

indications: Assessment of peripheral nerve lesions (e.g., foot drop from common peroneal nerve palsy, wrist drop from radial nerve injury).
Differentiation between neurapraxia, axonotmesis, and neurotmesis (S-D curves and chronaxie monitoring).
Monitoring nerve regeneration progress during rehabilitation.
Diagnostic evaluation of facial nerve function in Bell's Palsy.

general: Pacemaker, implanted cardioverter-defibrillator, or unstable cardiac conditions.
Inability of patient to tolerate electrical stimulation or cooperate with testing.
local: Over fresh surgical sites, active skin infections, or open wounds.
Over direct sites of tissue malignancy or vascular thrombosis.

patientPreparation: Explain that the test uses isolated mild electrical pulses to produce brief muscle twitches.
Reassure the patient that the pulses are temporary and will not cause sustained painful spasms.
apparatusPreparation: Ensure the diagnostic stimulator is set to constant current (CC) mode.
Verify the pulse duration selector is functioning, starting at 100 ms (analogous to infinite duration).
skinPreparation: Wash the testing skin area with soap and warm water to reduce resistance.
Moisten the active and dispersive electrode sponges with warm saline.
electrodePlacement: Position the dispersive electrode over an trunk or proximal site.
Place the active diagnostic pen electrode precisely over the motor point of the target muscle.
treatmentProcedure: Deliver a pulse at 100 ms duration and slowly raise the intensity until a minimal visible twitch is elicited. Record this value as the Rheobase.
Shorten pulse durations progressively (e.g., 50 ms, 30 ms, 10 ms, down to 0.01 ms), adjusting intensity at each step to maintain a threshold twitch. Record all values.
To find Chronaxie, double the recorded Rheobase intensity and locate the minimum pulse duration that still elicits a threshold twitch.

precautions: Avoid muscle fatigue during testing by keeping pulse intervals long (e.g., 2 seconds between pulses).
Ensure skin contact with the active pen electrode is consistent throughout testing; variations in hand pressure can skew results.
clinicalPearls: A kink in the S-D curve is a classic sign of partial denervation, representing a mix of healthy innervated fibers (responding at short durations) and denervated fibers (responding at longer durations). Tracking this kink over time helps monitor the progress of nerve regeneration.

clinicalProtocols.condition: Radial Nerve Wrist Drop Diagnostic Audit
clinicalProtocols.position: Forearm supported on table, wrist in neutral or slightly flexed
clinicalProtocols.electrodePlacement: Active pen electrode over the extensor digitorum communis motor point, dispersive electrode over the elbow
clinicalProtocols.frequency: Initially at onset of injury, and repeated once every 2 weeks to monitor progress
clinicalProtocols.purpose: Identify injury classification (axonotmesis vs. neurotmesis) and monitor recovery

vivaQuestions: Question: Define Rheobase and Chronaxie and state their normal clinical values. | Answer: Rheobase is the minimum current intensity (in mA or Volts) of infinite pulse duration required to elicit a threshold muscle contraction, with normal values between 2 and 8 mA. Chronaxie is the minimum pulse duration required to elicit a threshold contraction at double the rheobase intensity, with normal innervated tissue values between 0.05 and 0.5 ms. | Examiner Key Points: Definitions, units, infinite duration, double-rheobase relationship, normal ranges. | Clinical Importance: Clinically quantifies nerve excitability and detects denervation.

Question: How does the S-D curve change in response to complete denervation? | Answer: In complete denervation, the S-D curve shifts upward and to the right, becoming steep and straight with no kink. Short duration pulses (<1 ms) fail to elicit a response, and the chronaxie is prolonged above 10 ms. | Examiner Key Points: Upward/rightward shift, steep profile, absence of kink, chronaxie >10 ms. | Clinical Importance: Confirms complete loss of nerve supply, distinguishing it from partial lesions.

Question: What does a 'kink' in an S-D curve represent clinically? | Answer: A kink represents partial denervation and partial innervation. It occurs because the curve is a composite of healthy fibers (which respond to short-duration pulses) and denervated fibers (which only respond to long-duration pulses). | Examiner Key Points: Composite curve, partial denervation, shifts in kink position. | Clinical Importance: Confirms a partial nerve lesion and serves as an index of active nerve recovery.

Question: Why do myelinated nerves have a much shorter chronaxie than denervated muscle tissue? | Answer: Myelinated nerve axons have a high density of voltage-gated sodium channels at the Nodes of Ranvier, allowing rapid depolarization (chronaxie 0.05-0.5 ms). Denervated muscle fibers lack myelin and have slower channel kinetics, requiring longer pulse durations (10-50 ms) to depolarize. | Examiner Key Points: Nodes of Ranvier, sodium channel density, myelin insulation. | Clinical Importance: Explains the selective stimulation of nerve vs. muscle tissue.

Question: What is the Galvanic-Faradic test and what are its diagnostic limitations? | Answer: The Galvanic-Faradic test is a qualitative assessment comparing a muscle's response to long-duration galvanic (100 ms) and short-duration Faradic (1 ms) current. It is simple but qualitative and subjective, making it less reliable than quantitative S-D curve assessments. | Examiner Key Points: Galvanic (100 ms) vs Faradic (1 ms), qualitative nature, modern limitations. | Clinical Importance: Provides a quick bedside screening tool, though quantitative tests are preferred for detailed assessment.

Question: Explain the Accommodation Index and its values in innervated versus denervated tissue. | Answer: The Accommodation Index is the ratio of contraction threshold for slow-rising triangular pulses to that for rapid-rising rectangular pulses. Innervated muscles accommodate easily, yielding a ratio of 3 to 6. Denervated muscles accommodate poorly, yielding a ratio of 1 to 1.5. | Examiner Key Points: Ratio calculation, triangular vs. rectangular thresholds, normal vs denervated values. | Clinical Importance: Useful diagnostic indicator of active nerve supply.

Question: Why is constant current (CC) mode preferred over constant voltage (CV) mode for S-D curve plotting? | Answer: Skin resistance changes when electrodes are moved. CC mode automatically adjusts voltage to keep current stable, ensuring accurate threshold measurements. CV mode would allow current to fluctuate with resistance, skewing the curve. | Examiner Key Points: Skin resistance variations, active electrode movement, current stability. | Clinical Importance: Prevents diagnostic reading errors caused by skin resistance changes.

Question: During an S-D curve test, how do you prevent muscle fatigue from skewing results? | Answer: To prevent fatigue, keep the pulse frequency low (approx. 0.5 Hz, or 1 pulse every 2 seconds) and utilize the minimum current intensity necessary to elicit a threshold response. Avoid repetitive, rapid stimulation of the same muscle. | Examiner Key Points: Low pulse frequency, rest intervals, minimal threshold intensity. | Clinical Importance: Ensures consistent muscle responsiveness, preventing false denervation readings.

Question: How does cold skin temperature affect S-D curve readings and how is it managed? | Answer: Cold temperatures decrease nerve conduction velocity and muscle excitability, fictitiously raising the rheobase and prolonging the chronaxie. This is managed by pre-warming the limb to a normal skin temperature prior to testing. | Examiner Key Points: Cold effects on conduction/excitability, false readings, pre-warming. | Clinical Importance: Eliminates thermal confounding variables for accurate readings.

Question: What is the clinical significance of an S-D curve kink shifting progress to the left during serial testing? | Answer: A kink shifting progressively to the left indicates active nerve regeneration, as more muscle fibers are re-innervated and regain responsiveness to shorter pulse durations. | Examiner Key Points: Leftward shift, re-innervation, serial testing. | Clinical Importance: Provides objective, reassuring evidence of nerve recovery.

mcqs.question: What is the primary objective of clinical electrodiagnostics?
options: To evaluate peripheral nerve and muscle excitability
mcqs.answer: To evaluate peripheral nerve and muscle excitability
mcqs.explanation: Electrodiagnostics evaluates peripheral nerve and muscle excitability to diagnose neuromuscular pathology.

mcqs.question: Which parameter represents the minimum current intensity of infinite duration required to produce a threshold contraction?
mcqs.explanation: Rheobase is the minimum current intensity of infinite duration (represented clinically by a 100 ms pulse) required to produce a threshold contraction.

mcqs.question: Chronaxie is determined by measuring the threshold pulse duration at:
mcqs.explanation: Chronaxie is the pulse duration required to elicit a threshold response at exactly twice the rheobase intensity.

mcqs.question: What is the typical chronaxie range for a healthy, fully innervated muscle-nerve unit?
mcqs.explanation: Innervated nerves depolarize rapidly, resulting in a short chronaxie between 0.05 and 0.5 ms.

mcqs.question: A chronaxie value of 25 milliseconds in human skeletal muscle is indicative of:
mcqs.explanation: Denervated muscles show significantly prolonged chronaxie values, typically between 10 and 50 ms.

mcqs.question: Which graphical feature in an S-D curve represents partial denervation?
options: A shift down and to the left
mcqs.explanation: A kink in the S-D curve indicates a composite response from a mix of innervated and denervated muscle fibers, confirming partial denervation.

mcqs.question: According to the principle of S-D curves, denervation causes the curve to shift:
mcqs.explanation: Denervation increases excitability thresholds, shifting the S-D curve upwards and to the right (representing higher intensity and longer duration requirements).

mcqs.question: The Accommodation Index is calculated as the ratio of threshold current between:
mcqs.explanation: The Accommodation Index is the ratio of contraction threshold for slow-rising triangular pulses to that for rapid-rising rectangular pulses.

mcqs.question: What is the normal Accommodation Index value for fully innervated tissue?
mcqs.explanation: Healthy innervated tissues accommodate easily to slow-rising currents, yielding a normal ratio of 3 to 6.

mcqs.question: A completely denervated muscle yields an Accommodation Index of:
mcqs.explanation: Denervated muscles have lost their accommodation capacity, yielding an index near 1 (typically 1 to 1.5).

mcqs.question: Which electrical diagnostic test is qualitative and compares responses to Galilean and Faradic inputs?
mcqs.explanation: The Galvanic-Faradic test is a qualitative, classical assessment comparing muscle twitches in response to galvanic and Faradic inputs.

mcqs.question: Why is constant current (CC) mode preferred for S-D curve logging?
options: It maintains stable current delivery despite changes in skin resistance
It selectively stimulates sensory nerve endings
It eliminates the need for electrode pads
mcqs.answer: It maintains stable current delivery despite changes in skin resistance
mcqs.explanation: CC mode automatically adjusts voltage to maintain a stable current, correcting for changes in skin resistance and ensuring accurate diagnostic readings.

mcqs.question: What pulse duration is clinically used to measure Rheobase?
mcqs.explanation: Rheobase represents threshold intensity at infinite duration, represented clinically by pulses of 100 ms or 300 ms.

mcqs.question: Nerve injury without structural axonal degeneration is called:
mcqs.explanation: Neurapraxia is a transient conduction block without axonal degeneration or loss of structural continuity.

mcqs.question: In axonotmesis, the axon degenerates. This process is called:
mcqs.explanation: Wallerian degeneration is the process of axonal degeneration distal to the site of injury.

mcqs.question: What is the typical pulse frequency used during diagnostic S-D curve assessments?
mcqs.explanation: Low frequency pulses (around 0.5 Hz) prevent muscle fatigue and allow precise evaluation of individual twitches.

mcqs.question: A patient with Bell's Palsy is referred for testing. S-D curve evaluation of the facial nerve should be performed on the:
options: Both affected and unaffected sides for comparison
Trunk of the facial nerve only
mcqs.answer: Both affected and unaffected sides for comparison
mcqs.explanation: Testing both sides provides a healthy baseline for comparison, helping to control for anatomical and skin variations.

mcqs.question: How does pre-warming a cold limb affect diagnostic S-D curve accuracy?
options: It standardizes nerve excitability, preventing false prolongations of chronaxie
It accelerates Wallerian degeneration during testing
It converts the curve into a flat line
mcqs.answer: It standardizes nerve excitability, preventing false prolongations of chronaxie
mcqs.explanation: Standardizing skin temperature standardizes nerve conduction and excitability, preventing skewing of diagnostic parameters.

mcqs.question: Which of the following describes the electrical response in completely denervated muscle during Galvanic-Faradic testing?
options: Responds to Faradic but not galvanic current
Responds to galvanic but not Faradic current
Fails to respond to either current
mcqs.answer: Responds to galvanic but not Faradic current
mcqs.explanation: Denervated muscle lacks myelinated pathways and has a high chronaxie, failing to respond to short-duration Faradic pulses (1 ms) but responding to long galvanic pulses (100 ms).

mcqs.question: The active diagnosing electrode is typically styled as a:
options: Metal plate wrapped in dry linen
Diagnostic pen with a threshold button
mcqs.answer: Diagnostic pen with a threshold button
mcqs.explanation: A diagnostic pen electrode allows precise focus of current density over the target motor point.

mcqs.question: What happens to the chronaxie as a regenerating nerve re-innervates a muscle?
options: It progressively shortens toward normal limits
mcqs.answer: It progressively shortens toward normal limits
mcqs.explanation: During nerve regeneration and muscle re-innervation, chronaxie progressively shortens back toward normal values (<1 ms).

mcqs.question: The resting membrane potential of a muscle cell is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: The resting membrane potential of skeletal muscle is negative and typically around -90 mV.

mcqs.question: Which biological structure enters skeletal muscle at the anatomical motor point?
mcqs.explanation: The motor point is the site where the motor nerve branch penetrates the epimysium of the muscle belly.

mcqs.question: A complete rupture of a peripheral nerve with loss of perineurium continuity is classified as:
mcqs.explanation: Neurotmesis is the most severe nerve injury, involving complete rupture and disruption of all supportive connective tissue layers.

mcqs.question: To locate a deep-seated paretic muscle's motor point, the diagnostic pen electrode should use:
mcqs.explanation: Using short-duration pulses at appropriate intensity helps identify motor points while maintaining patient comfort by avoiding sustained contractions.

mcqs.question: Excitability of denervated skeletal muscle tissue is primarily due to:
options: Spread of current along muscle sarcolemma directly
mcqs.answer: Spread of current along muscle sarcolemma directly
mcqs.explanation: Lacking nerve supply, denervated muscle fibers must be depolarized directly along their sarcolemma, which has slower kinetics.

mcqs.question: The time interval between individual electrical diagnostic evaluation pulses is typically:
mcqs.explanation: An interval of 2 to 3 seconds prevents muscle fatigue and allows physiological recovery between twitches.

mcqs.question: The 'kink' in a partial denervation S-D curve represents the transition between:
options: Nerve fiber and direct sarcomere muscle fiber stimulation
mcqs.answer: Nerve fiber and direct sarcomere muscle fiber stimulation
mcqs.explanation: The kink represents the transition from nerve-mediated responses (at short durations) to direct muscle-membrane responses (at longer durations).

mcqs.question: Which type of nerve fiber has the fastest conduction velocity?
mcqs.explanation: Large myelinated A-alpha fibers have the fastest conduction velocity (up to 120 m/s), followed by A-beta fibers.

mcqs.question: If an S-D curve shifts to the left during serial testing, this represents:
options: Successful nerve regeneration and muscle re-innervation
mcqs.answer: Successful nerve regeneration and muscle re-innervation
mcqs.explanation: A leftward shift indicates active nerve recovery and muscle re-innervation, as thresholds improve and respond to shorter durations.

reference: Nelson & Currier - Clinical Electrotherapy, 3rd Edition - Chapter on Electrodiagnostics
Low & Reed - Electrotherapy Explained: Principles and Practice, 4th Edition - Chapter on Diagnostics
Jagmohan Singh - Textbook of Electrotherapy, 3rd Edition - Chapter 4
